story.title: Fan-Out Split — Obfuscation via Dispersion
story.narrative: This story investigates a **fan-out split** pattern: an illicit transaction (32054179) that distributes funds to multiple downstream recipients in a single hop. By splitting the balance across several outputs, the sender increases the cost of following each trail individually. This is a common obfuscation technique — harder to trace than a simple peel chain because the analyst must follow each branch independently and determine whether they eventually reconverge.

steps.title: The splitter node
steps.description: Transaction 32054179 is an illicit node (class = 1) with out-degree = 3. Instead of forwarding funds to a single destination, it splits the output to three separate transactions: 219542395, 218547008, and 33174163. This fan-out pattern suggests deliberate dispersion.

steps.title: Follow each branch
steps.description: All three downstream neighbors are currently unlabeled (class = 3 / unknown), meaning the ML model has low confidence on their status. Each branch may lead to different laundering endpoints — some may reconverge, some may exit through exchanges, and some may hit dead ends. The analyst must trace each independently.

steps.title: Branch depth analysis
steps.description: Extending the trace 2 hops deeper reveals whether the branches reconverge (suggesting a circular pattern) or diverge toward separate endpoints. Fan-out followed by fan-in is a hallmark of tumbling services. Here all three branches remain unknown, making this an AVERAGE difficulty case — the structural signal is clear but the labels are ambiguous.

steps.title: Upstream context
steps.description: Tracing upstream from the splitter reveals where the funds originated. If the upstream chain shows a peel pattern feeding into this fan-out, it confirms a multi-stage laundering pipeline: peel chain → split → dispersal. The upstream analysis completes the investigation picture.
